terms.definition: The national body that administers the registration of health practitioners, working with the Medical Board of Australia for doctors.
The standards body that assesses international medical graduates and runs the AMC examinations.
A doctor whose primary medical qualification was awarded by a training institution outside Australia or New Zealand.
An IMG who holds a specialist qualification obtained overseas and is seeking specialist registration in Australia.

terms.abbr: Computer Adaptive Test, multiple-choice question exam
terms.definition: The AMC's knowledge exam on the standard pathway, taken before the clinical assessment.
An AMC-accredited alternative to the AMC clinical exam, completed in an approved workplace under supervision.
A medical regulator/body in a country whose certification Australia recognises (UK, Ireland, USA, Canada, New Zealand), enabling the competent authority pathway.
Registration that lets a doctor complete a required period of supervised practice before applying for general registration.
Registration for a specific position or purpose, often used for area-of-need or postgraduate training roles.
Section 19AB of the Health Insurance Act 1973 restricts when IMGs can access Medicare provider numbers, generally for up to 10 years from first registration.
Areas identified as having a shortage of GPs; working in a DPA can satisfy the 19AB requirement for general practitioners.
The equivalent of a DPA for non-GP specialists for the purposes of Medicare access.
A 7-category scale (MM1 major city to MM7 very remote) used to target rural health workforce programs and incentives.
A healthcare-specific English test accepted by the Medical Board, alongside IELTS Academic, PTE Academic and TOEFL iBT.

terms.abbr: Office of the Migration Agents Registration Authority
terms.definition: Where to find a registered migration agent for personalised, regulated migration advice.
The main employer-sponsored temporary work visa (which replaced the TSS visa); commonly used to sponsor doctors.
